name: Pantanal Wetlands
dominantVegetation: Tropical wetland, gallery forest, cerrado grassland, floodplain
seasonalPattern: The dry season (July–October) lowers water levels across the floodplain, exposing vast areas of dry vegetation. In drought years, fires — often set for pasture management — spread uncontrollably across the parched landscape. The 2020 fires burned approximately 30% of the entire Pantanal, the worst disaster in the wetland's recorded history.
description: The Pantanal — the world's largest tropical wetland spanning Brazil, Bolivia, and Paraguay — has experienced catastrophic fires in recent years. The 2020 fire season destroyed approximately 30% of the 150,000 km² biome, killing millions of animals including jaguars, giant otters, and hyacinth macaws. Reduced river flows due to upstream deforestation and drought, combined with agricultural land conversion, have made this once-waterlogged ecosystem increasingly vulnerable to fire. The Pantanal's fire crisis highlights how even wetlands are not immune to climate-driven fire risk.
